script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Tuesday — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio closed zero point two one percent down on the session. A mixed tape across the primary holdings, with a handful of meaningful decliners pulling against some quiet gainers. Here is what you need to know.

Apple is suing OpenAI over trade secrets, and OpenAI is pushing back hard — calling the complaint without merit. More than four hundred former Apple employees have reportedly joined OpenAI, and the legal battle now directly threatens OpenAI's hardware ambitions at a moment when the AI device race is accelerating.

No broad macro data was available for Tuesday's session. Markets traded largely on earnings momentum and sector rotation, with the Nasdaq finishing the session in positive territory — up roughly zero point four percent — as investors rotated out of software names and into select hardware and consumer plays. That divergence showed up clearly inside this portfolio.

The headline story of Tuesday's session belongs to Apple and its escalating legal confrontation with OpenAI. Apple filed a lawsuit alleging that OpenAI misappropriated trade secrets, citing the movement of more than four hundred former Apple employees to OpenAI as central to its complaint. Apple is seeking damages and legal remedies that could directly constrain OpenAI's ability to develop hardware devices and continue aggressive hiring. OpenAI responded by stating it sees no evidence that the lawsuit has merit, though it acknowledged taking the complaint seriously. The timing matters. OpenAI has been pushing into hardware — an area Apple considers core to its competitive identity — and this litigation could create meaningful friction in that development pipeline. For holders of Apple in this portfolio, it is a story that blends legal risk with competitive signaling. Apple is defending its talent and its intellectual property simultaneously. Watch how the court proceedings develop and whether OpenAI's hardware timeline begins to show any delays as a result of this legal pressure.

Nvidia closed at two hundred eleven dollars and eighty cents, up zero point four zero percent on the session. Nvidia was a relative bright spot in an otherwise choppy tape for technology hardware. Analyst commentary flagged a broad investor rotation away from software companies and toward hardware makers — a trend that provided underlying support for the name Tuesday. Separately, a data point worth monitoring: New York State has moved to ban new artificial intelligence data centers, with fourteen additional states reportedly considering similar restrictions. That is a regulatory overhang that could affect the long-term demand trajectory for Nvidia's accelerator chips. Watch for any legislative developments in those fourteen states over the coming weeks.

Apple closed at three hundred fourteen dollars and eighty-six cents, down zero point one five percent on the session. The OpenAI litigation dominated the narrative here, as covered in the top story. OpenAI's public pushback — calling the lawsuit without merit — signals this dispute is heading toward a prolonged legal process rather than a quick settlement. The position in this portfolio remains the second-largest holding, so any material developments in this case will carry weight. Watch for any court filings or injunction attempts in the days ahead.

Turning to Alphabet, which closed at three hundred fifty-nine dollars and fifty-one cents, up zero point six five percent — making it one of the stronger performers among the primary holdings on Tuesday. Alphabet outperformed its index on the session and drew attention from institutional commentary, with the name appearing in Ray Dalio portfolio analysis and cited alongside a fourteen point five billion dollar data center investment in India that helped drive a forty-four percent jump in foreign direct investment into that country in twenty twenty-five. On the regulatory front, the UK Competition and Markets Authority has imposed new conduct requirements on Google's search business, mandating data portability for UK users within three to six months. That is a structural challenge to Google's search dominance in the region and worth tracking as a potential template for other jurisdictions. Watch whether the UK order triggers similar regulatory action in the European Union.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred eighty-three dollars and twelve cents, up zero point three nine percent on the session. Micron drew analyst attention Tuesday from two angles. Wedbush Securities flagged Micron's recently disclosed financial stake in a wafer supplier as a signal that the artificial intelligence memory supply chain could be approaching a bottleneck — characterizing it as an early warning that demand may soon outpace production capacity. Separately, a comparative analysis pitting Micron against SK Hynix in the AI memory race highlighted both companies as leading beneficiaries of the current AI hardware buildout. Micron had recently entered technical bear market territory before rebounding — the recovery continues to hold. Watch the wafer supply story closely, as any confirmation of a bottleneck could reprice the entire memory sector.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-four dollars and ninety-three cents, down zero point zero four percent on the session — essentially flat. The New York data center ban mentioned in the Nvidia context is equally relevant here. Microsoft has significant data center infrastructure exposure, and any state-level regulatory movement restricting new builds could affect the pace of its artificial intelligence infrastructure expansion. A quiet session for the name, but the regulatory backdrop deserves attention. Watch for any federal response to the state-level data center restrictions.

Over at Amazon, which closed at two hundred forty-seven dollars and forty-nine cents, up zero point eight eight percent — the strongest gain among the primary holdings on Tuesday. No specific news drove the move, but Amazon outperformed meaningfully relative to the broader session. The consumer discretionary and cloud infrastructure tailwinds appear to have provided support. This remains one of the larger primary positions and its relative strength on a mixed tape is worth noting.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred forty-eight dollars and thirteen cents, down one point seven five percent on the session — one of the softer performers among the primary names Tuesday. No company-specific news drove the decline. The move appeared to reflect broader sector pressure on semiconductor names outside of the direct artificial intelligence accelerator leaders. Watch for any product or competitive announcements that could reset sentiment.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-six dollars and eighteen cents, down two point eight four percent — the largest single-day decline among the primary holdings on Tuesday. No company-specific news was attributed to the move. Tesla has been sensitive to broader risk-off rotations and any macro pressure on consumer discretionary names. With the stock now below four hundred dollars, watch whether technical support at current levels holds.

Intel closed at one hundred seven dollars and seventy-six cents, down one point eight nine percent on the session. Intel continues to face structural competitive pressure across its product lines, and Tuesday's decline extended a difficult recent stretch for the name. No specific catalyst was identified for the move. Watch for any developments tied to Intel's foundry business, which remains the key long-term pivot the company is executing against.

Three data points to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any court response or injunction filing in the Apple versus OpenAI trade secret case — given the size of the Apple position in this portfolio, legal escalation could move the stock. Second, any legislative updates from the fourteen states reportedly considering data center restrictions — this touches Nvidia, Microsoft, and Alphabet simultaneously. Third, monitor the Micron wafer supply story for any analyst upgrades or additional commentary from Wedbush — a confirmed bottleneck narrative could reprice AI memory names across the sector.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch the Apple litigation closely — how OpenAI responds in court, and whether any injunctive relief is sought, could set the tone for the broader AI hardware competition narrative heading into the back half of the week.
